It will be argued that a breakthrough of ‘absolutism’ within both political theory and rational philosophy took place around the middle of the seventeenth century, 1 as exemplified in the work of Hobbes and Descartes, who, beneath their apparent differences and disagreements, shared to a substantial degree a common problem, to which they offered structurally similar solutions. These similarities can be spelled out by linking thought to experience, and to experience of a certain kind: human reactions to liminality. This chapter thus seeks to exemplify the general thesis that certain elaborations of liminal periods may prove decisive for the way in which a new order is established, at both the personal and societal levels, and that modernity on that account can in a crucial way be understood as a permanentizing of liminal conditions. Liminality in Political History: Linking Histories of the State with Histories of the Self Crudely speaking, the conceptual modelling of state and self are to be understood together because state formation is empirically related to a history of self- formation. The argument sustained in this chapter builds on literature that has analysed this very connection. In the Civilising Process (1994), Norbert Elias elegantly demonstrated how a specific kind of self-formation within the European court societies produced a fundamental and wide-ranging transformation of Western culture, which would affect the transformed, monopolized political rule called absolutism. As Oestreich (1982) showed in his analysis of the early modern state, the new characteristic of military discipline of the absolutist state grew directly out of the writings of the neo-stoic philosophers, and their concern with discipline, constancy and prudence. From the seventeenth century, the stoics gained new importance in the European history of ideas because they were interpreted as representing a pragmatic methodology of order, a secularized ethic of obedience brought to function, not by oppression, but by an active form of life 1 We had better make explicit that the term ‘absolutism’ (in inverted commas) is here and in the following invoked as referring jointly to state and self. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 117 conduct through self-restraint. The ideas and practices of stoicism spread within both monarchies and republics, and within the principalities and cities composing the Holy Roman Empire, in the process of transforming themselves into fully-fledged states. At this critical moment in Western history, the stoics were read with renewed attention because of the perceived similarity between the public disturbances of ancient Rome and those of modern Europe. In this process, the political concern with order and the formation of subjectivity became interlinked (see Szakolczai 1999). 2 Both Hobbes and Descartes must be read with this linkage in mind. The widespread experience of disorder is crucial to the interpretation of Hobbes and Descartes. As used here, this amounts to something more than a ‘contextual reading’ of political ideas, if this is taken to mean the social and cultural ‘contexts’ in which, and from which, ideas develop. The transformation of the modern Western state system was, from the very outset, related to a process of social disciplining. The structural need for social disciplining arose in a large yet very specific context, namely with the dissolution of the mediaeval world order, and the collapse of Western Christianity as a representative of unity and order. The Reformation and its subsequent religious schisms and the religious wars that followed created a political and epistemological chaos that governed from the early sixteenth to the mid seventeenth centuries, when Europe was plagued by international war, civil strife and endless religious disputes. The absolutist states arose as a structured response to this prevailing disorder. As argued in previous chapters, such historical periods must indeed be thought of as truly liminal. Reality is experienced as contingent and uncertain, with lasting forms yet to be made. The anxiety and doubt that characterizes liminality is ultimately overcome by shaping the subject, who through a series of tests is brought to reflect on his or her self, in the search of a new identity and role to take up on the return to his or her society. There are indeed historical periods in which the social order is very fundamentally questioned and where widespread infra- communal violence and lack of social hierarchy renders normal interpersonal relations difficult if not impossible. This was a social collapse, culminating in a prolonged period of permanent religious and civil wars. The suggestion made here is that the early seventeenth century must be understood as a liminal period, 2 The work of Gerhard Oestreich and Elias’ analysis of the Civilizing Process share many resemblances with the analysis of subjectivity and subjectivation made by Michel Foucault. For example, when Foucault developed the concept of governmentality towards the end of his life, this was not due to any sudden shift of attention towards political theory or political rule per se, but because Foucault considered new forms of political rationality as reformulating the question of being. In Foucault’s own words, he saw the ‘technologies of self’ in early modern and modern Europe as being linked to the ‘political technologies of individuals’ underlying political rule (Foucault 1988, 1991). The preoccupation with constraint or self-control, as emphasized also by Elias, was transferred from private or fairly closed circles of human interaction to the wider society. Liminality and the Modern 118 out of which concrete practices and ideas emerged as answers to the crisis. Following Borkenau, it was those answers and their routinization form the critical underpinning of a modern world-view. The concept of liminality has its relevance to political and social theory precisely here, as it was developed by anthropologists to make sense of human experience and processes of subjectivation during moments of dissolution, in other words, during social and political crises; and such a perspective is equally applicable to the history of thought. The concept of liminality helps to bring into perspective the otherwise strange and very radical turn to self-foundation, evident in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.
